title: How to Get Health Insurance as an Early Retiree (Before Medicare)

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Control your healthcare costs by architecting your income—drawing from post-tax accounts first—to strategically lower your MAGI and maximize ACA premium subsidies.
Never maintain a US-based ACA plan while living abroad, as it violates residency requirements and can force you to repay thousands in subsidies.
Fortify your finances by budgeting for your plan's full out-of-pocket maximum in a dedicated cash fund, treating it as your true annual financial risk.
Deploy your existing Health Savings Account (HSA) as a tax-free war chest to pay for major medical costs after you are no longer eligible to make new contributions.
summary: The staggering cost of health insurance before Medicare is a critical, often unmanaged, financial risk for early retirees. To solve this, the core advice is to proactively manage costs through a three-phase strategy: first, architect your income to maximize ACA subsidies; second, design a compliant coverage plan based on your global location; and finally, fortify your finances with tools like an HSA. By following this playbook, you transform healthcare from an uncontrollable liability into a managed expense, securing your financial freedom against unforeseen medical events.
ai_overview_answer: The most effective strategy for managing early retirement health insurance is to first architect your income to maximize ACA subsidies, then select a coverage plan based on your geographic location. Before choosing a plan, strategically manage your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI) by first drawing from post-tax accounts and then using controlled Roth conversions to qualify for the largest possible premium tax credits. For US-based retirees, a lower MAGI (below 250% of the Federal Poverty Level) makes a Silver plan with Cost-Sharing Reductions ideal, while a higher MAGI favors a Bronze plan paired with a Health Savings Account (HSA). If retiring abroad, you must secure a dedicated international health plan, as maintaining an ACA plan while living overseas is a compliance violation that can require you to repay subsidies. Financially, use an existing HSA as a tax-free fund to cover major medical costs and budget for your plan's out-of-pocket maximum in a separate savings account. COBRA should only be used as a tactical, short-term bridge to ensure continuous coverage between plans, not as a long-term solution due to its high cost.
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

@graph.description: The staggering cost of health insurance before Medicare is a critical, often unmanaged, financial risk for early retirees. To solve this, the core advice is to proactively manage costs through a three-phase strategy: first, architect your income to maximize ACA subsidies; second, design a compliant coverage plan based on your global location; and finally, fortify your finances with tools like an HSA. By following this playbook, you transform healthcare from an uncontrollable liability into a managed expense, securing your financial freedom against unforeseen medical events.

author.name: Ben Carter
author.description: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

step.name: Architect Your Income for Maximum Control
step.text: The critical first step is to move from defense to offense. Instead of treating insurance premiums as a fixed cost to be paid, you must see them as a variable outcome you can directly influence. For the early retiree, the most powerful tool for managing healthcare costs is not picking the perfect plan—it's architecting your income to control your eligibility for ACA Marketplace subsidies. This is financial engineering. The objective is to strategically and legally reduce your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI), the key figure that determines your eligibility for thousands of dollars in annual premium tax credits. The cornerstone of this approach is the "Withdrawal Waterfall" strategy. Think of your retirement assets not as one big pool, but as distinct buckets with different tax treatments. The sequence in which you draw from them is paramount.

step.name: Design Your Global Coverage Blueprint
step.text: With your income architecture established, the next critical variable is geography. Mastering your MAGI is a powerful offensive move for managing costs in the US, but that strategy is dangerously incomplete for a Global Professional. Your geographic footprint dictates your compliance reality and coverage options. Applying a US-centric strategy to a global retirement is a catastrophic error. Instead, you must build a resilient, location-aware blueprint based on whether you are a US-based retiree, a stationary expat, or a nomadic retiree.

step.name: Fortify Your Financial Defenses
step.text: Once your coverage blueprint is locked in, the final phase is to build the financial guardrails that make it unbreakable. Choosing the right plan manages logistical risk; fortifying your finances ensures that a health event will never become a financial catastrophe. This involves weaponizing your Health Savings Account (HSA), budgeting for the out-of-pocket maximum, and using COBRA only as a tactical, short-term bridge.

mainEntity.name: How do I structure my income for maximum ACA subsidies?

acceptedAnswer.text: The objective is to strategically lower your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI). The most effective tactic is a "withdrawal waterfall": 1. First, spend non-MAGI assets: Use cash from savings and the principal (cost basis) from post-tax brokerage accounts. These do not count as "income" for subsidy calculations. 2. Next, manufacture income via Roth conversions: Convert funds from a traditional IRA/401(k) to a Roth IRA. The converted amount counts as MAGI, but this lets you create the precise income needed to optimize your subsidy while moving money into a tax-free growth vehicle. 3. Last, draw from tax-deferred accounts: Only then should you take withdrawals from traditional 401(k)s or IRAs. These distributions count fully toward your MAGI and directly reduce your subsidies.

mainEntity.name: What is the best health insurance for an American retiring abroad?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your choice depends entirely on your lifestyle. For long-term residency in one country: A dedicated "expat health insurance" plan is the correct, compliant choice. These are comprehensive, long-term plans designed for those living abroad and are often required for residency visas. For a nomadic lifestyle across multiple countries: A "travel medical insurance" policy is more appropriate. These plans focus on covering emergencies, not routine care. For either option, you must ensure the policy includes a rider for coverage during visits back to the United States. Assuming your international plan automatically covers you on trips home is a significant and costly oversight.

mainEntity.name: Can I keep my ACA plan if I live overseas?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. This is a major compliance trap. ACA Marketplace plans are for residents of the US service area where the plan is offered. If you establish bona fide residency in another country, you are no longer eligible to enroll or receive subsidies. Attempting to maintain an ACA plan while living abroad long-term can require you to pay back any premium tax credits you received. The only secure strategy is to obtain a dedicated expat health plan.

mainEntity.name: How does an HSA work in early retirement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your Health Savings Account (HSA) transitions from a contribution vehicle to a powerful distribution tool. Once you are no longer covered by a qualifying High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP), you cannot add new funds. However, the existing balance is yours to use, tax-free, for any qualified medical expense. Think of it as your private, tax-advantaged medical emergency fund for deductibles, COBRA premiums, dental costs, and more. After age 65, you can withdraw money for any reason without penalty, though you will pay ordinary income tax on non-medical withdrawals, just like a traditional 401(k).

mainEntity.name: What are my options if I retire at 62?

acceptedAnswer.text: Retiring at 62 means you have a three-year gap to bridge before Medicare eligibility. Your goal is to secure continuous, affordable coverage for this specific period. Your primary options are: COBRA: Continue your former employer's coverage for up to 18 months. It is expensive but provides seamless continuity. ACA Marketplace Plan: This is the most common and often most cost-effective solution, as you may qualify for significant subsidies based on your retirement income. Spouse's Plan: If your spouse is still working, joining their employer-sponsored plan may be a simple and effective option. Private Off-Marketplace Plan: You can purchase a plan directly from an insurer, but you will not be eligible for income-based subsidies.

body.content: Your CEO Playbook for Early Retirement Health Insurance

You’ve done it. Through years of disciplined execution, you’ve achieved the ultimate entrepreneurial exit: you’ve acquired your own freedom. Retiring early is a feat of strategic planning and relentless focus—the successful launch of your "Business-of-One." You have meticulously de-risked your investment portfolio to withstand market volatility. But the most significant threat to your new enterprise isn't a market downturn; it's a personal and complex liability that often goes unmanaged until it's too late: the staggering cost of healthcare.

For the CEO of a "Business-of-One," the gap between corporate-sponsored coverage and Medicare eligibility at age 65 is not a minor inconvenience. It is a multi-year, potentially six-figure operational risk loaded with compliance traps. Unsubsidized insurance on the open market can easily cost $1,000 per month or more, transforming healthcare from a line item into the single greatest challenge for an early retiree.

The standard advice—a simple menu of COBRA, private plans, and the ACA Marketplace—is dangerously inadequate for a Global Professional. Those guides fail to address the fundamental truth you know from your career: strategy must precede tactics. Choosing a plan without first architecting the income that dictates its cost is like choosing a factory location without analyzing the supply chain. It's a reactive posture in a game that demands proactive control.

This is not a menu of options. This is your executive playbook. This framework is a sequential, strategic process that moves from high-level architecture to tactical execution and, finally, to rigorous risk management. We will build a three-phase system to put you in absolute command of your healthcare costs and transform compliance anxiety into a feeling of complete control.

items: **Phase 1: Architect Your Income.** Before you look at a single insurance plan, you will design the financial foundation that dictates your costs. By strategically managing your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI), you will unlock the largest possible subsidies. Get this right, and you are no longer playing defense; you are setting the price you are willing to pay.

**Phase 2: Design Your Global Coverage Blueprint.** Only after engineering your income do you select your tool. Your physical location is the most critical variable. We will build a decision tree that leads to a compliant and effective blueprint, whether you are a US-based retiree, a stationary expat, or a global nomad, avoiding catastrophic compliance gaps that can jeopardize your freedom.

**Phase 3: Fortify Your Financial Defenses.** With your income architecture and coverage blueprint in place, the final phase is to build the financial guardrails that make your plan bulletproof. You will learn to deploy your HSA as a tax-free war chest and budget for your true financial exposure, ensuring a health event never becomes a financial catastrophe.

body.content: It’s time to stop treating healthcare as an uncontrollable expense and start managing it like the critical business division it is.
Phase 1: Architect Your Income for Maximum Control

The critical first step is to move from defense to offense. Instead of treating insurance premiums as a fixed cost to be paid, you must see them as a variable outcome you can directly influence. For the early retiree, the most powerful tool for managing healthcare costs is not picking the perfect plan—it's architecting your income to control your eligibility for ACA Marketplace subsidies. This is financial engineering. The objective is to strategically and legally reduce your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI), the key figure that determines your eligibility for thousands of dollars in annual premium tax credits.

The cornerstone of this approach is the **"Withdrawal Waterfall" strategy**. Think of your retirement assets not as one big pool, but as distinct buckets with different tax treatments. The sequence in which you draw from them is paramount.

items: **Drain Post-Tax & Cash Accounts First.** Your first source of funds should be cash reserves, savings, and non-qualified brokerage accounts. When you sell assets from a brokerage account, only the *gains* are added to your MAGI, not the entire withdrawal. This is the least MAGI-impactful way to fund your lifestyle.

**Execute Strategic Roth Conversions.** Next, you manufacture income in a controlled way. By converting funds from a traditional IRA or 401(k) to a Roth IRA, you create taxable income. The genius here is that you can convert just enough to fill up the lowest tax brackets without pushing your MAGI into a zone that eliminates your subsidies.

**Tap Tax-Deferred Accounts Last.** Only after exhausting more strategic options should you draw directly from traditional 401(k)s or IRAs. Every dollar from these accounts is treated as ordinary income, directly increasing your MAGI and reducing potential subsidies.

body.content: This level of control is precisely what financial experts advise. As Karl Schwartz, CPA, CFP® at Team Hewins, states, "If your investment portfolio includes a variety of asset types, you may have the flexibility to strategically reduce your MAGI during the years that you're buying health insurance through the exchange—increasing your tax credits and reducing your premiums."

Crucially, you must understand the current subsidy landscape. Thanks to the Inflation Reduction Act, the hard "subsidy cliff" at 400% of the Federal Poverty Level (FPL) has been temporarily removed through 2025. Instead, a "subsidy taper" gradually decreases assistance as your income rises. This change transforms MAGI management from a game of avoidance into a game of precision. Your MAGI is now a key performance indicator for your "Business-of-One." Your goal is to model your withdrawals and conversions to land at the optimal point on that taper, maximizing your subsidy and putting you in command of your healthcare balance sheet.
Phase 2: Design Your Global Coverage Blueprint

With your income architecture established, the next critical variable is geography. Mastering your MAGI is a powerful offensive move for managing costs in the US, but that strategy is dangerously incomplete for a Global Professional. Your geographic footprint dictates your compliance reality and coverage options. Applying a US-centric strategy to a global retirement is a catastrophic error. Instead, you must build a resilient, location-aware blueprint.
Blueprint A: The US-Based Retiree

If you remain in the United States, your strategy revolves around the ACA Marketplace. Your primary decision is selecting the correct "metal tier" based on the income you engineered in Phase 1. This isn't just about premiums; it's about optimizing your total potential cost.

rows: Lowest premiums and the ability to contribute to a tax-advantaged Health Savings Account (HSA).
Minimizes fixed costs (premiums) while building a dedicated, tax-free medical fund for future expenses. Best for those confident in their health who want to leverage the HSA's triple-tax advantage.
Unlocks powerful Cost-Sharing Reductions (CSRs), which are unavailable on any other tier.

Dramatically reduces your out-of-pocket risk by lowering deductibles and copayments. For those with a MAGI below 250% of the poverty line, a Silver plan is the only way to access this critical benefit.

body.content: Blueprint B: The Stationary Expat (e.g., Retiring in Portugal)

For those establishing long-term residence in a single foreign country, the primary compliance risk is improperly maintaining an ACA plan. Marketplace plans require you to live in the plan's US service area. Establishing bona fide residency abroad makes you ineligible and could force you to repay thousands in subsidies.

The correct strategy is to secure a robust, local-compliant international health plan. These plans are specifically designed for expatriates and provide comprehensive medical care in your new country. Top-tier providers like Cigna Global, GeoBlue, and AXA Global Healthcare offer policies that function like primary insurance, not temporary travel coverage. Crucially, ensure your plan includes a rider for emergency coverage during visits back to the United States.
Blueprint C: The Nomadic Retiree (e.g., "Sabbatical Year")

A nomadic lifestyle demands a clear distinction between long-term expat insurance and travel medical insurance. They are not interchangeable. Travel insurance is for short-term emergencies, designed to stabilize you before repatriation for ongoing care. It is not comprehensive health insurance.

For a multi-country journey, the appropriate tool is a high-quality travel medical policy designed for emergency care across various nations. You can layer this with a US-based catastrophic plan or strategically maintain COBRA for a limited time to ensure you have a backstop for significant medical events that might occur during brief trips home.
Address Compliance Head-On

Finally, you must understand the direct conflict between the ACA and the Foreign Earned Income Exclusion (FEIE). To claim the FEIE, you must prove to the IRS that you are a "bona fide resident" of a foreign country or meet the "physical presence test." This directly contradicts the residency requirement for an ACA plan. Attempting to claim both can jeopardize your tax status and create significant compliance headaches. Prioritizing a compliant global or travel plan based on your blueprint is the only way to manage this risk effectively.
Phase 3: Fortify Your Financial Defenses

Once your coverage blueprint is locked in, the final phase is to build the financial guardrails that make it unbreakable. Choosing the right plan manages logistical risk; fortifying your finances ensures that a health event will never become a financial catastrophe. This is how you secure true peace of mind. As certified financial planner Patrick Cummins notes, a frequent oversight is "inadequate planning for health care expenses... [Early retirees] often pay more for health insurance yet get less coverage." This phase counteracts that structural risk.

items: **Weaponize Your Health Savings Account (HSA):** Your ability to contribute to an HSA ends the moment you leave a qualifying high-deductible health plan. However, the existing balance transforms into your most powerful financial tool: a tax-free medical war chest. This is not for minor co-pays; it's a dedicated fund you can deploy to cover significant costs with a triple-tax advantage (tax-free contributions, growth, and withdrawals). You can use these funds to pay for deductibles, co-insurance, dental and vision expenses, or even staggeringly high COBRA premiums. This account is your first line of defense.

**Reframe Your Risk: Budget for the Out-of-Pocket Maximum:** Most people fixate on the monthly premium. This is a tactical error. Your premium is a predictable operating expense; your **out-of-pocket maximum** represents your true, total annual financial risk. You must plan for this worst-case scenario. The solution is a dedicated "healthcare sinking fund" in a liquid, high-yield savings account, holding enough cash to cover the full out-of-pocket maximum. By pre-allocating these funds, you transform a potential disaster into a manageable, planned-for expense.

**Use COBRA as a Tactical Bridge, Not a Long-Term Solution:** Viewing COBRA as a primary strategy is an incredibly expensive mistake. Its true value is not in its cost but in its continuity. For the Global Professional, COBRA is a tactical bridge—a short-term risk mitigation tool. It is the perfect instrument to cover a 1-3 month gap between leaving your job and the start of your new ACA or international plan. The cost is high, but it guarantees identical, uninterrupted coverage, which can be invaluable for a short, defined period. Deploy it only as a strategic, short-term solution.

body.content: Finally, a critical part of fortifying your plan is avoiding catastrophic assumptions. Many early retirees moving abroad assume a low cost of living translates to zero healthcare costs. While many countries have excellent public systems, expats often face waiting periods, are ineligible for full benefits, or find the public system insufficient. Securing a high-quality private international plan—often a requirement for residency visas—is essential for timely access to care. Failing to budget for robust private coverage is a planning error that can jeopardize both your health and your residency status.
Frequently Asked Questions

With the strategic foundation in place, flawless execution comes down to answering the specific, high-stakes questions that arise when your plan meets reality. Here are clear answers to the most common queries from Global Professionals on the verge of early retirement.

items:
**How do I structure my income for maximum ACA subsidies?**

The objective is to strategically lower your Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI). The most effective tactic is a "withdrawal waterfall":
1.  **First, spend non-MAGI assets:** Use cash from savings and the principal (cost basis) from post-tax brokerage accounts. These do not count as "income" for subsidy calculations.
2.  **Next, manufacture income via Roth conversions:** Convert funds from a traditional IRA/401(k) to a Roth IRA. The converted amount counts as MAGI, but this lets you create the precise income needed to optimize your subsidy while moving money into a tax-free growth vehicle.
3.  **Last, draw from tax-deferred accounts:** Only then should you take withdrawals from traditional 401(k)s or IRAs. These distributions count fully toward your MAGI and directly reduce your subsidies.

**What is the best health insurance for an American retiring abroad?**

Your choice depends entirely on your lifestyle.
*   **For long-term residency in one country:** A dedicated **"expat health insurance"** plan is the correct, compliant choice. These are comprehensive, long-term plans designed for those living abroad and are often required for residency visas.
*   **For a nomadic lifestyle across multiple countries:** A **"travel medical insurance"** policy is more appropriate. These plans focus on covering emergencies, not routine care.

For either option, you must ensure the policy includes a rider for **coverage during visits back to the United States**. Assuming your international plan automatically covers you on trips home is a significant and costly oversight.

**Can I keep my ACA plan if I live overseas?**

No. This is a major compliance trap. ACA Marketplace plans are for residents of the US service area where the plan is offered. If you establish bona fide residency in another country, you are no longer eligible to enroll or receive subsidies. Attempting to maintain an ACA plan while living abroad long-term can require you to pay back any premium tax credits you received. The only secure strategy is to obtain a dedicated expat health plan.

**How does an HSA work in early retirement?**

Your Health Savings Account (HSA) transitions from a contribution vehicle to a powerful distribution tool. Once you are no longer covered by a qualifying High-Deductible Health Plan (HDHP), you cannot add new funds. However, the existing balance is yours to use, tax-free, for any qualified medical expense. Think of it as your private, tax-advantaged medical emergency fund for deductibles, COBRA premiums, dental costs, and more. After age 65, you can withdraw money for any reason without penalty, though you will pay ordinary income tax on non-medical withdrawals, just like a traditional 401(k).

**What are my options if I retire at 62?**

Retiring at 62 means you have a three-year gap to bridge before Medicare eligibility. Your goal is to secure continuous, affordable coverage for this specific period. Your primary options are:
*   **COBRA:** Continue your former employer's coverage for up to 18 months. It is expensive but provides seamless continuity.
*   **ACA Marketplace Plan:** This is the most common and often most cost-effective solution, as you may qualify for significant subsidies based on your retirement income.
*   **Spouse's Plan:** If your spouse is still working, joining their employer-sponsored plan may be a simple and effective option.
*   **Private Off-Marketplace Plan:** You can purchase a plan directly from an insurer, but you will not be eligible for income-based subsidies.

body.content: Conclusion: You Are the CEO of Your Healthcare Future

Answering these tactical questions solidifies your operational readiness, but now you must resume your rightful role. As the CEO of your "Business-of-One," navigating the pre-Medicare gap is your final, most critical strategic project. This is no longer a passive expense to be endured; it is an active system to be managed.

By embracing this mindset, you fundamentally change your relationship with one of the biggest risks in early retirement. You are no longer a consumer reacting to volatile healthcare costs. You are the executive making intentional choices. You now have the complete playbook to:

items: **Architect** your income like a CFO, strategically managing withdrawals to control your MAGI and maximize subsidies.
**Design** your global coverage blueprint like a COO, selecting the compliant, location-appropriate insurance that eliminates risk whether you're in Boise or Bali.
**Fortify** your finances like a risk manager, deploying your HSA and budgeting for your out-of-pocket maximum to neutralize worst-case scenarios.

body.content: This framework is designed to move you from anxiety to execution. You have the tools and the strategic clarity. You have transformed your greatest source of uncertainty into a well-managed component of your hard-earned freedom. Lead from the front.
